blurb: Small rounded blue-green pads budding fresh pads off their edges, studded with tufts of fine glochids rather than long spines. Species still to pin down — a classic little prickly pear in the making.

care.light: Full sun keeps the pads plump and compact and stops new growth from stretching thin.
care.water: Soak-and-dry — a bit more while it's actively pushing pads in summer, then dry through winter.
care.temperature: Warm in summer; keep above ~35°F and dry when cold (this one's ID is unconfirmed, so I'm treating it as tender).
care.dormancy: A summer grower; rest cool and dry in winter.
care.feeding: A dilute cactus feed once or twice in summer.
care.potting: Repot with tongs or folded newspaper — never bare hands. Pads snap off easily as new plants.
care.propagation: Lay a detached pad on dry mix to callus, then pot; it roots readily.
care.bloom: Cup-shaped flowers along the pad rims on mature plants in strong light.
care.troubles: The glochids are the main hazard to you; the plant itself mainly fears overwatering rot and cochineal scale.
care.toxicity: Non-toxic, but the tiny barbed glochids lodge in skin and are miserable to remove — site it where pets and people won't brush against it.

sources.title: Opuntia (Prickly Pear) — NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox
